[Speaker Notes] Enzyme names

Oxidation of glucose to CO 2 is a 24-electron oxidation
The electrons from glucose oxidation feed into the electron transport pathway, driving synthesis of ATP.
CO 2

Hans Krebs Showed That the Oxidation of Acetate is Accomplished by a Cycle
The TCA cycle is known as the Krebs cycle – and also as the citric acid cycle
Pyruvate (3C) from glycolysis is oxidatively decarboxylated to acetate
Loss of 1 C as CO 2
Then acetate is degraded to 2XCO 2 in the TCA cycle
Some ATP is produced
More NADH is made
NADH goes on to make more ATP in electron transport and oxidative phosphorylation
Figure 19.1 The Tricarboxylic Acid Cycle

The Citric Acid Cycle A. Overview of the Citric Acid Cycle
1. Acetyl CoA (2 C) + 4 C = 6 C (citrate)
Steps 3 & 4: 2 carbons are lost as CO 2
Steps 3, 4, 6 & 8: NADH and FADH2 are produced
Step 5: GTP is produced
1 GTP = 1 ATP (energy)

The Tricarboxylic Acid Cycle
For One Acetyl CoA
TCA cycle produces:
2 CO 2
3 NADH  = 3x 2.5 ATP
1 FADH 2 =      1.5 ATP
1 GTP     =         1 ATP
Total ATP =     10 ATP
TCA cycle provides carbon for:

The Citric Acid Cycle

19.2 How Is Pyruvate Oxidatively Decarboxylated to Acetyl-CoA?
Pyruvate must enter the mitochondria to enter the TCA cycle
Oxidative decarboxylation of pyruvate is catalyzed by the pyruvate dehydrogenase complex
Pyruvate dehydrogenase is a noncovalent assembly of three enzymes
Five coenzymes are required

Pyruvate Dehydrogenase (PDH)
Multi-subunit enzyme complex
CoA-SH + NAD + Pyruvate  Acetyl-CoA + NADH + CO 2
Regulation:
Allosterically activated by Fructose1,6-bisPhos.
Inhibited by Acetyl-CoA and NADH
PDH kinase (converts PDH to phospho-PDH):
Phospho-PDH = less active
Activated by NADH, acetyl –CoA
Inhibited by NAD, CoA-SH
PDH phosphatase:
Activated by Ca++ (muscle contraction)

Pyruvate Dehydrogenase is a Multi-Subunit Complex of Three Enzymes
Figure 19.3 (b) Model of human PDC: a truncated version of E2

Pyruvate Dehydrogenase is a Multi-Subunit Complex of Three Enzymes
Figure 19.3 (c)
Model of the E2/E3BP:E3 core complex of human pyruvate dehydrogenase

Pyruvate Dehydrogenase (PDH)
Glycolysis

The Coenzymes of the Pyruvate Dehydrogenase Complex
Coenzyme A
Derived from vitamin B5 (pantothenic acid)
Acts as “acyl carrier”
Provides a handle for transferring acyl groups
TCA cycle reactions
Fatty Acid degradation
Thiol is good neucleophile and good leaving group
Citrate synthase

Citrate Synthase
Acetyl-CoA + oxaloacetate  citrate + CoA
C α of the acetyl group in acetyl-CoA is acidic and can be deprotonated to form a carbanion
This carbanion is a strong nucleophile that can attack the α -carbonyl of oxaloacetate, yielding citryl-CoA
Thioester hydrolysis then produces citrate

The citrate synthase synthase reaction initiates the TCA cycle
C α of the acetyl group in acetyl-CoA is acidic
Resulting from thioester from CoA
Carbanion attacks carbonyl of oxaloacetate
Formation of Carbon-Carbon bond
Combining 2C acetyl group with 4 C oxaloacetate
Result is 6 C molecule: citrate (tricarboxylic acid)
CoA is recycled
Tricarboxylic acid

Aconitase
Aconitase makes isocitrate (an isomer of citrate)
Makes it easier for the following step, which results in loss of CO 2
Movement of –OH group

Isocitrate dehydrogenase
Isocitrate dehydrogenase (IDH) catalyzes the loss of 1 C as CO2 in a redox step
1 C is oxidized to CO2, NAD is reduced to NADH
This is a decarboxylation step
Product:
α-ketoglutarate

Isocitrate Dehydrogenase Catalyzes the First Oxidative Decarboxylation in the Cycle
Figure 19.10 The isocitrate dehydrogenase reaction.  Hydride removal by NAD + is followed by a
β -decarboxylation reaction that expels the central carboxyl group as CO 2

a -Ketoglutarate Dehydrogenase Catalyzes the Second Oxidative Decarboxylation of the TCA Cycle
a -Ketoglutarate Dehydrogenase catalyzes the loss of 2nd C as CO 2 in a redox (decarboxylation) step
NAD is reduced to NADH
Product: Succinyl CoA (4C molecule)
Rxn requires CoA

a -Ketoglutarate Dehydrogenase Catalyzes the Second Oxidative Decarboxylation of the TCA Cycle
Like pyruvate dehydrogenase, a -ketoglutarate dehydrogenase is a multienzyme complex. The complex uses five different coenzymes.

Succinyl-CoA synthase
Succinyl-CoA synthase catalyzes the conversion of Succinly-CoA to succinate
Free phosphate can be used to produce GTP (energy)
Takes advantage of CoA being a good leaving group

19.4  How Is Oxaloacetate Regenerated to Complete the TCA Cycle?
Hydrolysis of succinyl-CoA (a CoA ester) drives the phosphorylation of GDP to produce GTP

19.4  How Is Oxaloacetate Regenerated to Complete the TCA Cycle?
Succinyl-CoA Synthetase Catalyzes
a Substrate-Level Phosphorylation
A nucleoside triphosphate is made
This is possible because succinyl-CoA is a high-energy intermediate
Its hydrolysis (the hydrolysis of a CoA ester) drives the phosphorylation of GDP to produce GTP

Succinyl-CoA Syn. mechanism
The mechanism of the succinyl -CoA synthetase reaction.
Phosphate anion carries out a nucleophilic attack on the carbonyl C of succinyl -CoA, forming succinyl phosphate ( phosphorolysis )
Subsequent phosphoryl transfer to an active-site His forms a phosphohistidine intermediate, releasing the product succinate
The phosphoryl group is then transferred to GDP to produce GTP

Completion of the TCA Cycle – Oxidation of Succinate to Oxaloacetate
This process involves a series of three reactions that recycles oxaloacetate to restart the cycle
Rearranging one 4C unit into another
Succinate to oxaloacetate
These reactions include:
Oxidation of a single bond to a double bond
FAD is reduced to FADH2
Hydration across the double bond
Oxidation of the resulting alcohol to a ketone
NAD reduced to NADH

Final 3 steps
1. Succinate conversion to Fumarate is used to reduce FAD to FADH2
2. Water is added to fumarate (alkene) to form Malate
3. Malate conversion to oxaloacetate is used to reduce NAD to NADH
Oxaloacetate is recycled, NADH, FADH2 are produced (more energy)

Succinate Dehydrogenase Is FAD-Dependent
An FAD-dependent oxidation of a single bond to a double bond
The mechanism involves hydride removal by FAD and a deprotonation
This enzyme is actually part of the electron transport pathway in the inner mitochondrial membrane
The electrons transferred from succinate to FAD (to form FADH 2 ) are passed directly to ubiquinone (UQ) in the electron transport pathway
The Succinate Dehydrogenase Reaction

Fumarase Catalyzes the trans -Hydration of Fumarate to Form L -Malate
Hydration occurs across the newly formed double bond
Hydration involves trans -addition of the elements of water across the double bond
The actual mechanism is not known for certain
The Fumarase Reaction
The Malate Dehydrogenase Reaction

19.7 – Can the TCA Cycle Provide Intermediates for Biosynthesis?
The TCA cycle provides several of these
α -Ketoglutarate can be used to make glutamate, which can be used to make purine nucleotides, Arg and Pro, as well as neurotransmitters
Succinyl-CoA can be used to make porphyrins (heme rings)
Fumarate and oxaloacetate can be used to make several amino acids and also pyrimidine nucleotides
Oxaloacetate can be used for gluconeogenesis
Note that mitochondrial citrate can be exported to be a cytoplasmic source of acetyl-CoA for Fatty acid synthesis

Question
NADH made during glycolysis in the cytoplasm is used for the electron transport chain in the mitochondria. Which of the following systems is used for transport of NADH from the cytoplasm to the mitochondria?
Citrate shuttle
Malate shuttle
Oxaolacetate suttle
Carnitine shuttle
Airport Shuttle

The Malate Shuttle
Malate

TCA cycle
Goes in as Malate, comes out as Aspartate, Ox-ac: intermediate

Gluconeogenesis substrates
Many molecules can be used as substrates for gluconeogenesis
Pyruvate, oxaloacetate, lactate, glyceraldehyde
All amino acids except for Leucine and Lysine
Acetyl-CoA is NOT a substrate for gluconeogenesis
Why?
Acetyl-CoA already lost one C as CO 2 from pyruvate
Although Acetyl-CoA can be used to make oxaloacetate through TCA, it loses 2 more C’s
Net zero carbons, no new material can be made!

The Citric Acid Cycle Acetyl-CoA not a substrate for gluconeogenesis
By the time acetyl-CoA goes around the cycle, it’s already lost all its carbons
No net gain, so effectively, Acetyl-CoA cannot “produce” oxaloacetate
However, pyruvate can be directly converted to oxaloacetate for gluconeogenesis
